# Thread: Greetings from the Desert Southwest in the US
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/greetings-from-the-desert-southwest-in-the-us.28346/>
**Subforum:** Meet and Greet
**Posts:** 8
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### mtnhiker — 2023-11-20T19:32:37+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/greetings-from-the-desert-southwest-in-the-us.28346/post-328483>

Hi Everyone,

I recently encountered Dr. Kruse's work through Rick Rubin's podcast and that opened my eyes to the beautiful landscape that you all are exploring here.
I've been exploring red light therapy in recent months and honestly not making much headway due to my mysterious reactions to it along with natural sunlight exposure. I'm finding that even short doses of red light + NIR from a RLT panel or natural sunlight can kick up significant irritability and depression. This reaction seems to have developed in the last two years after a confirmed mold exposure. I also have a lingering heavy metal issue as well. Couple that with imbalanced methylation at present and I'm not the best detoxifier.

Putting aside speculation on the underlying cause, I'm trying to find a way in that doesn't magnify the irritability / depression too much and thought I'd see if anyone has suggestions. With the red light panel (RedRush 400), a 1 minute dose of continuous red light and NIR at a 3 feet distance still seems to be too much. The next experiment I'm thinking of trying there is to focus solely on the NIR.

With sun exposure, I was trying 20 minute doses toward the end of the day when UVB was gone but UVA was still present. The next step there might be to simply dial that dose way back.

I've been struggling with gut issues for a long time and am curious about how sunlight exposure might help with gut motility. If I'm reading the literature on that correctly, it seems like midday sun could have a profound effect on the microbiome and I hope on motility as well. Curious if anyone has seen a relationship between motility and light exposure on the gut.

That's enough for now. Grateful to be here and for the opportunity to learn in community!

Chris

### Saichi — 2023-11-20T20:40:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/greetings-from-the-desert-southwest-in-the-us.28346/post-328484>

First step anyone should take is mitigate the nnemf toxins in one's living spaces. Unfortunately many people even here never do or never do to any meaningful degree.

### JanSz — 2023-11-20T20:48:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/greetings-from-the-desert-southwest-in-the-us.28346/post-328485>

>
> Putting aside speculation on the underlying cause, I'm trying to find a way in that doesn't magnify the irritability / depression too much and thought I'd see if anyone has suggestions. The next experiment I'm thinking of trying there is to focus solely on the NIR.
>
> With sun exposure, I was trying 20 minute doses toward the end of the day when UVB was gone but UVA was still present. The next step there might be to simply dial that dose way back.
>

# Thread: Hi from Northern NM
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hi-from-northern-nm.28356/>
**Subforum:** Meet and Greet
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Amethyst — 2023-11-23T06:04:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hi-from-northern-nm.28356/post-328562>

I am new to all of this but super enthusiastic, it all makes so much sense. Very appreciative of the information! I am about at latitude 36, and 6000 feet elevation. Lots of sun in the summer, and I was out in it a lot, never liked sunscreen. But winter is much less. Some simple questions:
1\. Is early dawn light, before sunrise valuable, or just when I see rays? Same at dusk?
2\. What about when there is a lot of chemtrail?
3\. I used to do sungazing, and am starting that again, slowly, maybe 5 minutes at dawn and again at dusk, along with slow 5 second in and 5 second out breaths. Any advice pro or con?
4\. Are incandescent red bulbs ok (not painted red ones) or are LED better? Is there a time limit for how long is advisable to use red light at night?
Thanks!

### Anthony Smith — 2023-11-23T09:22:26+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hi-from-northern-nm.28356/post-328569>

Your #3 . . . it is my understanding this is a VERY good thing, especially with bare feet on the ground.

### Joseph LeBlanc — 2023-11-28T20:46:11+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hi-from-northern-nm.28356/post-328743>

> [Amethyst said:](/goto/post?id=328562)
>
> 1\. Is early dawn light, before sunrise valuable, or just when I see rays? Same at dusk?

Light from the sun at any point in time has benefits, even the light in twilight periods. And the benefits are numerous, even if you are not in direct rays.

> [Amethyst said:](/goto/post?id=328562)
>
> 2\. What about when there is a lot of chemtrail?

I currently get sun regardless of the amount of geoengineering trails left in the sky.

Although, I do wonder if maybe there could be detriments if a certain type of particle is pumped into the atmosphere that would selectively block the healing lower frequency IR rays. Seems to me like this could potentially be a concern.

> [Amethyst said:](/goto/post?id=328562)
>
> Is there a time limit for how long is advisable to use red light at night?

The best advice is least amount of light of any kind after sunset bc **all light**, even dim red light, **will suppress melatonin to some degree**.

### Joseph LeBlanc — 2023-11-28T22:57:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hi-from-northern-nm.28356/post-328755>

> [Joseph LeBlanc said:](/goto/post?id=328743)
>
> The best advice is least amount of light of any kind after sunset bc **all light**, even dim red light, **will suppress melatonin to some degree**.

I need to correct myself here

From Dr. Kruse: "red light at night does not suppress melatonin, it does RAISE cortisol"

So:
The best advice is least amount of light of any kind after sunset bc **all light**, even dim red light, **will disrupt circadian rhythms to some degree**

# Thread: I am visiting El Salvador  Jan 3 though the 17th with my wife and staying in the Atami escape resort. Would love to get together with other members wh
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-am-visiting-el-salvador-jan-3-though-the-17th-with-my-wife-and-staying-in-the-atami-escape-resort-would-love-to-get-together-with-other-members-wh.28383/>
**Subforum:** Meet and Greet
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Heisenburg — 2023-12-01T22:43:34+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-am-visiting-el-salvador-jan-3-though-the-17th-with-my-wife-and-staying-in-the-atami-escape-resort-would-love-to-get-together-with-other-members-wh.28383/post-328837>

I will be visiting El Salvador with my wife for the first time January 3 through the 17th and staying at the Atami escape resort. Would like to get together with members who will be down there at that time frame. I imagine navigating through a new country and culture will be a steep learning curve and would love any advise and information to help. thanks Steve turner

### NikkiYork — 2023-12-01T23:56:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-am-visiting-el-salvador-jan-3-though-the-17th-with-my-wife-and-staying-in-the-atami-escape-resort-would-love-to-get-together-with-other-members-wh.28383/post-328838>

> [Heisenburg said:](/goto/post?id=328837)
>
> I will be visiting El Salvador with my wife for the first time January 3 through the 17th and staying at the Atami escape resort. Would like to get together with members who will be down there at that time frame. I imagine navigating through a new country and culture will be a steep learning curve and would love any advise and information to help. thanks Steve turner

We were just there. It’s not that hard. Get the Waze app, it shows you the way best and plan on having trouble finding a location, because they have very few signs to find things. We often needed to ask people, all friendly and nice, but don’t all speak English.

Atami is a great place to start and venture from. We liked their restaurant, Pelicanos, but food was overall very good everywhere.

Some stuff you can only access with 4 wheel drive so make sure you know, if there is such a street because otherwise it can get dicey. Like in the mountains. The main roads are all good and mostly brand new.

# Thread: Geo engineering?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/geo-engineering.28401/>
**Subforum:** Meet and Greet
**Posts:** 12
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Evee — 2023-12-08T15:18:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/geo-engineering.28401/post-328982>

So after six weeks in Mexico (almost the entire time in Playa Del Carmen, Quintana Roo) I’m back in New Jersey because I’m still working on permanently moving away from this area, and one thing that struck me is I never saw a sky that looks like this in Quintana Roo.
I did see some streaky sky in Merida, which is Yucatán, but is this Geo engineering that I’m looking at in New Jersey?
I’ll research this myself as well, but does anyone already know - Is there a database of places that are not allowing Geo engineering in their skies?

And this picture is nothing compared to what I saw over the summer in New Jersey, sometimes I would see streaks so dramatic, that I could also see the shadow behind them

### DaveAlfa — 2023-12-09T03:19:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/geo-engineering.28401/post-329016>

Yes, it is Geo-engineering ! Nice shot! I’d also like to know if there’s a list of areas that don’t have Geo-engineering?

### JanSz — 2023-12-09T03:31:39+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/geo-engineering.28401/post-329017>

> [Evee said:](/goto/post?id=328982)
>
> Is there a database of places that are not allowing Geo engineering in their skies?

There is no definitive database of places that are not allowing geoengineering in their skies, but there are some sources that provide information on the status and regulation of geoengineering research and activities around the world.

For example, you can find a list of countries that have agreed to ban geoengineering as part of a UN treaty to protect biodiversity [here](https://cen.acs.org/articles/88/i45/Countries-Agree-Ban-Geoengineering.html).

You can also read about how **Mexico has recently prohibited solar geoengineering,** citing environmental and regulatory concerns [here](https://www.usatoday.com/story/news/factcheck/2023/03/07/fact-check-mexico-banning-solar-geoengineering-not-chemtrails/11382941002/).

Additionally, you can learn about how the U.S. blocked a UN resolution on geoengineering in 2010, arguing that it would undermine the scientific basis for climate action [here](https://www.scientificamerican.com/article/u-s-blocks-u-n-resolution-on-geoengineering/).

Geoengineering is a controversial practice that aims to combat global warming by reflecting sunlight back to space or enhancing carbon sinks. However, it also poses significant risks and uncertainties for the environment and human society.
Therefore, many experts and activists oppose geoengineering and advocate for more effective and sustainable solutions to address climate change.

### JanSz — 2023-12-09T03:46:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/geo-engineering.28401/post-329018>

There are many sources that provide information on the status and regulation of geoengineering research and activities around the world. Here are some of the most relevant and recent ones:

-   \[Preparing the United States for security and governance in a geoengineering future\]: This article from the Brookings Institution discusses the potential risks and uncertainties of geoengineering, as well as the need for improved understanding, detection, monitoring, regulation, and deterrence of geoengineering activities. It also proposes a five-principle framework for international cooperation on geoengineering governance.
-   \[The regulation of geoengineering: A gathering storm for international climate change policy?\]: **This paper from the Centre for Defence and Security Law** examines the legal and ethical implications of geoengineering, as well as the challenges and opportunities for international law to address them. It argues that an international agreement should be urgently established to regulate decisions regarding the testing and use of geoengineering.
-   \[Geoengineering Map explainer\]: **This interactive map from Geoengineering Monitor provides an overview of existing, planned, completed, and cancelled geoengineering projects, experiments, and research around the world.** It also includes information on funding sources, governance structures, public engagement, and environmental impacts.
-   \[Geoengineering-Governance\]: This webpage from Umweltbundesamt (German Federal Environment Agency) provides a **summary of Germany’s position on geoengineering governance.** It outlines Germany’s support for a precautionary approach to geoengineering research and development, as well as its involvement in various international initiatives on this topic.
-   [New Report Says U.S. Should Cautiously Pursue Solar Geoengineering Research to Better Understand Options for Responding to Climate Change Risks](https://www.nationalacademies.org/news/2021/03/new-report-says-u-s-should-cautiously-pursue-solar-geoengineering-research-to-better-understand-options-for-responding-to-climate-change-risks): This report from the National Academies of Sciences, Engineering, and Medicine recommends that the U.S. should lead a solar geoengineering research program across federal agencies and scientific disciplines. [It also suggests that solar geoengineering should be considered only as a last resort option after reducing greenhouse gas emissions significantly](https://www.nationalacademies.org/news/2021/03/new-report-says-u-s-should-cautiously-pursue-solar-geoengineering-research-to-better-understand-options-for-responding-to-climate-change-risks)[1](https://www.nationalacademies.org/news/2021/03/new-report-says-u-s-should-cautiously-pursue-solar-geoengineering-research-to-better-understand-options-for-responding-to-climate-change-risks).

### Evee — 2023-12-09T15:26:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/geo-engineering.28401/post-329026>

> [JanSz said:](/goto/post?id=329018)
>
> There are many sources that provide information on the status and regulation of geoengineering research and activities around the world. Here are some of the most relevant and recent ones:
>
>
> -   \[Preparing the United States for security and governance in a geoengineering future\]: This article from the Brookings Institution discusses the potential risks and uncertainties of geoengineering, as well as the need for improved understanding, detection, monitoring, regulation, and deterrence of geoengineering activities. It also proposes a five-principle framework for international cooperation on geoengineering governance.
> -   \[The regulation of geoengineering: A gathering storm for international climate change policy?\]: **This paper from the Centre for Defence and Security Law** examines the legal and ethical implications of geoengineering, as well as the challenges and opportunities for international law to address them. It argues that an international agreement should be urgently established to regulate decisions regarding the testing and use of geoengineering.
> -   \[Geoengineering Map explainer\]: **This interactive map from Geoengineering Monitor provides an overview of existing, planned, completed, and cancelled geoengineering projects, experiments, and research around the world.** It also includes information on funding sources, governance structures, public engagement, and environmental impacts.
> -   \[Geoengineering-Governance\]: This webpage from Umweltbundesamt (German Federal Environment Agency) provides a **summary of Germany’s position on geoengineering governance.** It outlines Germany’s support for a precautionary approach to geoengineering research and development, as well as its involvement in various international initiatives on this topic.
> -   [New Report Says U.S. Should Cautiously Pursue Solar Geoengineering Research to Better Understand Options for Responding to Climate Change Risks](https://www.nationalacademies.org/news/2021/03/new-report-says-u-s-should-cautiously-pursue-solar-geoengineering-research-to-better-understand-options-for-responding-to-climate-change-risks): This report from the National Academies of Sciences, Engineering, and Medicine recommends that the U.S. should lead a solar geoengineering research program across federal agencies and scientific disciplines. [It also suggests that solar geoengineering should be considered only as a last resort option after reducing greenhouse gas emissions significantly](https://www.nationalacademies.org/news/2021/03/new-report-says-u-s-should-cautiously-pursue-solar-geoengineering-research-to-better-understand-options-for-responding-to-climate-change-risks)[1](https://www.nationalacademies.org/news/2021/03/new-report-says-u-s-should-cautiously-pursue-solar-geoengineering-research-to-better-understand-options-for-responding-to-climate-change-risks).

Have no problem with agreeing on eating fish or shellfish, and other food which mostly could be meats from animals.
\-------------
But I like to think about supercentenarians.
That is my goal, if I make it that long or not.

Average Americans eat 220 pounds of meat yearly.
Supercentenarians 20 pounds a year.
That visually comes to one thick slice of bacon daily.
That slice of bacon could be a fish or shrimp or animal.

So, after that "slice of bacon" we are done worrying about DHA & EPA.
Good idea to think about of what to eat next, to get to that about 1500 calories.
What that part of food is?
.

### JanSz — 2024-02-12T17:07:41+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello.28581/post-331370>

> [coho3131 said:](/goto/post?id=331365)
>
> I wonder if the quercetin in the **rosemary** is actually what is helping you.
>
> [@Brent Patrick](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/19843/) and I like drinking teas made from herbs that contain quercetin.

**Rosemary**, the fragrant evergreen herb native to the Mediterranean, offers a delightful addition to both culinary dishes and potential health benefits.

1.  **Antioxidants and Anti-Inflammatory Properties**:
    -   Rosemary is rich in antioxidants and anti-inflammatory compounds, which play a crucial role in neutralizing harmful particles called free radicals. [These compounds are believed to boost the immune system and improve blood circulation](https://www.medicalnewstoday.com/articles/266370)[1](https://www.medicalnewstoday.com/articles/266370).
    -   Laboratory studies have confirmed rosemary’s antioxidant content, making it a valuable addition to your diet.
2.  **Digestive Aid**:
    -   In Europe, rosemary has been traditionally used to treat indigestion. [Germany’s Commission E has even approved rosemary for this purpose, although scientific evidence remains limited](https://www.medicalnewstoday.com/articles/266370)[1](https://www.medicalnewstoday.com/articles/266370).
    -   While more research is needed, its historical use suggests potential benefits for digestion.
3.  **Enhanced Memory and Concentration**:
    -   The aroma of rosemary may positively impact cognitive function. [Research indicates that inhaling rosemary scent can improve concentration, performance, speed, accuracy, and mood](https://www.medicalnewstoday.com/articles/266370)[1](https://www.medicalnewstoday.com/articles/266370).
    -   So, next time you’re studying or working, consider having a sprig of rosemary nearby!
4.  **Neurological Protection**:
    -   Rosemary contains an ingredient called **carnosic acid**, which fights off damage caused by free radicals in the brain.
    -   [Studies in rats suggest that rosemary might be beneficial for people who have experienced a stroke](https://www.medicalnewstoday.com/articles/266370)[1](https://www.medicalnewstoday.com/articles/266370).
5.  **Other Potential Benefits**:
    -   [Rosemary has been associated with immune support, skin protection, hair nourishment, and even potential cancer prevention](https://botanicalinstitute.org/rosemary/)[2](https://botanicalinstitute.org/rosemary/)[3](https://www.organicfacts.net/health-benefits/herbs-and-spices/rosemary.html)[4](https://www.naturalfoodseries.com/11-benefits-rosemary/).
    -   It’s also a good source of iron, calcium, and vitamin B-6.

\===========
Yes, **rosemary** does contain **quercetin**, a flavonoid with several potential health benefits
\============
**other flavonoids**

**Rosemary** contains several other flavonoids, which contribute to its potential health benefits:

1.  [**Diosmetin**: A flavone found in rosemary, diosmetin has antioxidant properties and may contribute to overall health](https://www.drugs.com/npp/rosemary.html)[1](https://www.drugs.com/npp/rosemary.html).
2.  [**Diosmin**: Another flavonoid present in rosemary, diosmin is known for its potential vascular protective effects](https://www.drugs.com/npp/rosemary.html)[1](https://www.drugs.com/npp/rosemary.html).
3.  [**Genkwanin**: This flavonoid has anti-inflammatory properties and may play a role in supporting health](https://www.drugs.com/npp/rosemary.html)[1](https://www.drugs.com/npp/rosemary.html).
4.  **Luteolin**: Luteolin is a flavone with antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties. [It’s found in rosemary and other plants](https://www.drugs.com/npp/rosemary.html)[1](https://www.drugs.com/npp/rosemary.html).
5.  [**Hispidulin**: Hispidulin is a flavonoid that may have various health benefits, including antioxidant and anti-inflammatory effects](https://www.drugs.com/npp/rosemary.html)[1](https://www.drugs.com/npp/rosemary.html).
6.  [**Apigenin**: Apigenin, commonly found in rosemary, has been studied for its potential anti-cancer properties and other health benefits](https://www.drugs.com/npp/rosemary.html)[1](https://www.drugs.com/npp/rosemary.html).

/////////////////////

1.  **Dosage and Safety**:

    -   While rosemary is nutritious, it’s not suitable for everyone. **Avoid it during pregnancy**, as it can stimulate menstrual flow.
    -   [For memory enhancement, a trial with university students used **500mg of rosemary twice daily for one month**, showing promise in increasing memory performance and reducing anxiety and depression](https://botanicalinstitute.org/rosemary/)[2](https://botanicalinstitute.org/rosemary/).
    -   [Testing various doses, researchers found that a **750mg/day dose** increased long-term memory recall, while a high-dose group taking **6,000mg/day** experienced memory decline](https://botanicalinstitute.org/rosemary/)[2](https://botanicalinstitute.org/rosemary/).
    -   **[**Traditional uses include 2g of chopped leaf infused in water or 2 to 4g of the shoot**](https://www.drugs.com/npp/rosemary.html)**[3](https://www.drugs.com/npp/rosemary.html).****

In summary, enjoy rosemary in moderation as part of your diet.
It’s a flavorful addition, but like any herb, too much can have adverse effects.
Bon appétit!

# Thread: Itchy arms
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/itchy-arms.28602/>
**Subforum:** Meet and Greet
**Posts:** 15
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Thomas Russsell — 2024-02-13T01:05:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/itchy-arms.28602/post-331383>

Hello,
I am new to this forum, but am a long time reader and believer of Jack Kruse’s work. Almost 2 years ago, while my wife and I were building a wall I accidentally elbowed her, quite forcefully, just above her forehead. She immediately felt pain in the back of her skull where it meets her spine, and tingling down her shoulders and arms. She still experiences pain near the base of her skull some days, and most terribly, extreme itching in one or both arms almost every day. The only thing that calms the itch is ice packs on the arms. She has tried ozone shots to the cervical area and acupuncture, both expensive and did not cure her, though may have provided a bit of temporary relief. She just started going to a chiropractor, who thinks adjustments and more time are the answer. I am looking for any other suggestions that may help.
Thank you,
Tom.

### coho3131 — 2024-02-13T01:34:43+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/itchy-arms.28602/post-331387>

Low redox and intracellular dehydration?

How many hours per day does she spend outside? How many hours in front of a screen? How much ALAN?

What is her fasting blood sugar?

Or better yet, does she have signs of leptin resistance?

I hope she feels better soon.

She has tried ozone shots to the cervical area and acupuncture, both expensive and did not cure her, though may have provided a bit of temporary relief. She just started going to a chiropractor, who thinks adjustments and more time are the answer. I am looking for any other suggestions that may help.
> Thank you,
> Tom.

**Tom, I am sorry your wife has been in pain and experiencing extreme arm itching.**
**I like the questions coho3131 asked.** Also is your wife eating _**Epi-Paleo**_, low carb? Or keto or carnivore?

I personally suffer only mild upper arm itching (usually one arm or the other) when I am exposed to some fabrics or foods I am sensitive to...tough for me... got sensitive to a whole lot of things after a cancer health crisis 14 years ago.

May not be related... but I have also heard of people going Keto diet /low carb and Keto rashes

### Are You Itching on a Ketogenic Diet? – Dr. Berg on Keto Rash [**https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Od7LYWhcLFs**](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Od7LYWhcLFs) ​

AND even better video...

### Ep:23 Keto Rash, Dr. Cywes You tube

One thing I've learned the hard way is that healthy building materials is an oxymoron...could the wall materials be a factor?
_Good brainstorming always throws out some crazy ideas._ ​

**I hope and pray you get exactly the help you need.**

### NikkiYork — 2024-02-13T08:35:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/itchy-arms.28602/post-331389>

> [Thomas Russsell said:](/goto/post?id=331383)
>
> Hello,
> I am new to this forum, but am a long time reader and believer of Jack Kruse’s work. Almost 2 years ago, while my wife and I were building a wall I accidentally elbowed her, quite forcefully, just above her forehead. She immediately felt pain in the back of her skull where it meets her spine, and tingling down her shoulders and arms. She still experiences pain near the base of her skull some days, and most terribly, extreme itching in one or both arms almost every day. The only thing that calms the itch is ice packs on the arms. She has tried ozone shots to the cervical area and acupuncture, both expensive and did not cure her, though may have provided a bit of temporary relief. She just started going to a chiropractor, who thinks adjustments and more time are the answer. I am looking for any other suggestions that may help.
> Thank you,
> Tom.

Hi Tom,

That sucks, two years is a long time. I’m sorry this happened.
I can related to these rather freaky conditions and I am hoping that Jack’s regiments can help.

Since you’ve been reading for a while, you’ll know all about it.
I’ve been very successful with sun and in winter additional red light, so I can only emphasize that.

6 weeks of morning sun got the feeling on the back of my hand back. And it was supposedly a lost cause - cervical stenosis.

Too bad it’s winter. I nonetheless sit as much outside as I have time to do so. It seems to really make a difference.

Did she try light? I need to look up where you live. Best of luck and hopefully a speedier recovery.

### Thomas Russsell — 2024-02-14T00:54:03+0200

Thank you so much for all the advice and kind words. My wife and I are in good health, following an epi-paleo diet. Her fasting blood sugar is about 90. We turn the wifi off, have black-out curtains, and keep a temp of 62 degrees for sleeping, however, my wife works in a school with strong wifi and about 1,000 laptops, and is working on an online degree when she gets home, spending about 3 more hours online per evening for the past 2 years. We realize this is not optimal. From about 7 PM, we wear blue-blocking lenses. We have a 660 and 850 nm red light in the bathroom.
Because of the temporary relief my wife experienced from ozone shots to the cervical area and a misalignment diagnosed by her chiropractor, we are leaning toward a physical diagnosis of Brachioradial pruritis. We live in South Carolina and get as much sun as we can, which is not much at all on weekdays. In reading on Brachioradial pruritis, they say sun can be a cause. Any thoughts on this one?

About humidifier on Air type heating system.
The problem with humidifiers is that they work on home water system.
That water, at least in my area is relatively hard and clogs the humidifier within less than two weeks. Even if it went first thru water softener.
A month ago, I removed my old humidifier, told them to install the best available but also told them to install Reverse Osmosis system that feeds water to humidifier.

.....

Thanks to all for the much appreciated thoughtful and helpful ideas and suggestions.
Tom

# Thread: DHA & 'Cell Membranes'
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dha-cell-membranes.28614/>
**Subforum:** Meet and Greet
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### mike7.83 — 2024-02-18T00:08:37+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dha-cell-membranes.28614/post-331575>

There is constant mention of cell membranes in this forum but having read a lot of Gilbert Ling's work, he mentions that there are no cell membranes. What he did say was that cells are enclosed in multiple layers of structured water. A query that I have regarding 'essential' DHA. Have there been any studies performed on Mead acid for energy transfer in the brain and other tissues compared with DHA? DHA is docosahexaenoic acid 22: \[n-3\] 4,7,10,13,16,19 omega 3 polyunsaturated fatty acid. Mead acid aka eicosatrienoic acid 20: \[n-9\] 5,8,11 omega 9 polyunsaturated fatty acid. There seems to be a particular fetish for studying omega 3 & 6 polyunsaturated acids but very little meaningful study of omega 9 polyunsaturated fatty acids that humans can make in abundance. Is it possible that we do not need any omega 3s & 6s and can function fully on self-made as-needed omega 9s? Thanks.

> And I will get my BMI and fat percentage, is there anything else like that you suggest?

About PUFA.
All PUFA's are suspect.
Do not worry about too low DHA.
Worry about way way too high LA (Linoleic Acid)
PUFA (Omega-6 and Omega-3)
We used to have about the same amount of both.
That was in the time when people were hungry.
Say, before 1850.
Someone invented a way to squeeze seeds and get oil out of them.
Suddenly farmers were asked to produce 10000x more seeds, maybe more.
Everything eating seeds or seed oil grows, pigs, fish, birds, other animals.
People too.
First, they were thin (like you) but also short, about 5'4".
Now people are not hungry, they are growing up and wide.

We are mammals.
What happen in real world to animals.
End of summer and fall there is lots of food.
Animals eat a lot, the more they eat the hungrier they get (leptin is getting low).
Why do they do that?
They are getting ready to hibernate.
When they are about ready, but not really, they get into torpor.
Refresh your understanding of what torpor is (not pretty).
This is where more than half people on this earth are getting, getting into torpor.
People are getting ready to hibernate.
That is a price of not being hungry.

# Thread: El Salvador residency question
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/el-salvador-residency-question.28689/>
**Subforum:** Meet and Greet
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael Allred — 2024-03-15T22:11:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/el-salvador-residency-question.28689/post-332579>

We are seriously considering an El Salvador residency. Can anyone help me with the following two questions please:

1\. Is the residency temporary or permanent? If temporary, how long does it last?

2\. Is anything required to maintain your residency? For example, do you have to spend a specific amount of time of the year living there

### jackmck — 2024-03-16T17:25:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/el-salvador-residency-question.28689/post-332596>

Hi Michael,

Here's a few things I've found:

The process for becoming a permanent resident in ES includes becoming a temporary one first. Once a person has been a temporary resident for some time (for applicants from non-Spanish-speaking countries it's three years), they can apply for Permanent Residency. At that time, they can leave the country for up to two years in a single trip without losing their status. Once they become a citizen (which happens for most applicants after five years), they can leave the country for five years at a time, and special conditions regarding their country of origin apply.

During those first three years, Immigration will only allow temporary residents to leave the country for 90 days in a single trip or for 120 days cumulatively over the course of one year. This means that one needs to plan to live in El Salvador for at least eight months out of the year.


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
